slides.description: The slide features the title "AI Screenplays" in large blue text at the top. Below the title is a cartoon image, reminiscent of the South Park animation style, depicting a lavish office. In the foreground, an older man in a suit, resembling a Hollywood executive, stands and gestures. To his left, a cardboard robot labeled "A.W.E.S.O.M.-O" sits at a large wooden desk. The desk is adorned with several golden awards (including what appear to be Oscar and Emmy statues), a red doll labeled "CARTMAN", a telephone, a brown notebook, and a stack of papers with "SCRIPT" visible on the spine. A framed photograph of several people, including the executive, is also on the desk. In the background, a large window overlooks a sunset scene with mountains and a prominent "HOLLYWOOD" sign. To the right of the executive, a framed picture on the wall displays the text "AWESOME-O" with "LAME" in a red box below it. Also on the right side of the office are a globe, an aquarium containing an alien figure, and a safe. The overall image suggests a humorous or satirical take on AI's role in the film industry, referencing the South Park episode featuring AWESOM-O.

The slide is titled "Generating Long Documents" in large blue text. It presents four bullet points outlining the lecture's scope and objectives. The first bullet states, "AI can be used to generate long, coherent text documents." The second bullet provides examples: "Books, reports, screenplays." The third bullet specifies the day's activity: "Today we will build an agentic flow to generate screenplays for movies." The final bullet highlights the broader applicability of the method: "This flow architecture can be generalized to other long text documents." This slide transitions from the humorous introduction of AI screenplays on the previous slide to the practical application and methodology that will be covered in the lecture.

The slide is titled "Challenge: 'One-Shot' Long-Form" in large blue text. It outlines the difficulties associated with using AI to generate extensive documents. The first main bullet point states, "It is difficult for an AI to generate a long coherent document in one-shot." The second main bullet point elaborates on this, explaining, "Local vs global – the AI is good at writing small parts, but cannot maintain coherence across whole document." This point is further broken down into two sub-bullets: "– Becomes 'lost in the middle'" and "– Context drift – deep into the document, the AI can hallucinate or lose focus of what to write." This slide directly addresses the challenges of the task introduced in the previous slide, which focused on generating long documents like screenplays.

The slide is titled "Movie Screenplays" in large blue text. It presents two main bullet points detailing the elements and structure of screenplays relevant to AI generation. The first main bullet states, "Screenplays have many different elements the AI needs to capture," followed by three sub-bullets: "– Story shape," "– Goals of 'sequences' in the movie," and "– Scene action and dialogue." The second main bullet point is "Hierarchical structure of a screenplay," which is further broken down into four sub-bullets: "– Premise – basic elements of the movie," "– Story arc – high level plot structure," "– Sequences – more detailed plot," and "– Scenes – even more detailed plot." This slide builds upon the previous slide's discussion of the challenges of long-form generation by outlining the specific components an AI must manage when creating a screenplay.

The slide is titled "Screenplay Flow" in large blue text at the top. Below the title, a flowchart diagram illustrates the hierarchical process for generating a screenplay. The flow begins with a blue rectangular box labeled "Premise" with the text "premise.json" below it. An arrow points from "Premise" to a purple rectangular box labeled "Arc" with "arc.json" below it. From "Arc," two arrows branch out: one points to an orange rectangular box labeled "Sequences" with "sequence.json" below it, and another points downwards to a green rectangular box labeled "Scenes" with "scenes.json" below it. Each of the four boxes ("Premise," "Arc," "Sequences," "Scenes") also contains a small icon resembling a file cabinet or treasure chest. This diagram visually represents the hierarchical structure of a screenplay introduced in the previous slide, outlining the steps an AI agent might take to generate a script, starting from a premise and detailing it into arcs, sequences, and scenes, with each stage potentially corresponding to a JSON file.

The slide is titled "Premise Agent" in large blue text at the top. In the top right corner, a small image of the AWESOM-O robot from the first slide is visible. The slide contains four bullet points describing the function and output of the Premise Agent. The first bullet states, "A chat agent that discusses with you what your movie is about," followed by a sub-bullet listing key elements: "– Title, logline (like an executive summary), characters, genre, central conflict." The second bullet point explains, "You can ask the agent to give ideas for the plot if you are stuck." The third bullet notes, "This chat can take a few minutes even if you have a general sense about your movie (takes time to specify all the major elements of the movie)." The final bullet point specifies the agent's output: "This agent then creates a file premise.json." This slide elaborates on the initial 'Premise' stage of the 'Screenplay Flow' introduced in the previous slide, detailing the interactive process and specific outputs of the agent responsible for defining the movie's core concept.

The slide is titled "Premise.json" in large blue text at the top. Below the title, a code block displays the contents of an example `premise.json` file on a dark background. The JSON object contains several key-value pairs: "project_id": "shadow_of_the_dark_world_20260402_025456", "title": "Shadow of the Dark World", "writers": ["TBD"], "source_script": null, "format_notes": "Awesome-O generated this from a CLI chat.", "logline": "A seemingly ordinary young man, Link, is thrust into a perilous quest after his uncle is gravely wounded rescuing Princess Zelda. He must gather legendary artifacts, wield the fabled Master Sword, and confront a malevolent wizard to prevent the dark lord Ganon's release and the conquest of their world.", "genre": ["fantasy", "action", "adventure"], and "themes": ["hero's journey", "destiny", "courage in the face of overwhelming evil", "good versus evil", "sacrifice"]. This slide provides a concrete example of the output generated by the 'Premise Agent' discussed in the previous slide, illustrating the structured data that defines the core concept of a movie.

The slide is titled "Premise.json" in large blue text at the top. Below the title, a code block on a dark background displays a continuation of the example `premise.json` file from the previous slide. This portion of the JSON object details additional elements of the movie's premise. It includes the key-value pair: "central_conflict": "Link's race against time to understand and embrace his role as a hero, stopping the sorcerer Aghanim from sacrificing Princess Zelda and six other maidens, a ritual that would unleash the ancient evil Ganon from the corrupted Dark World upon the Light World.". It then defines the "setting" object, which contains "primary_time": "High fantasy era, contemporary-feel for characters" and an array of "primary_places": ["Hyrule Castle", "Kakariko Village", "Eastern Palace", "Lost Woods", "Dark World"]. Finally, it shows the beginning of the "protagonists" array, with the first protagonist defined as an object containing "name": "Link", "role": "Unassuming hero, destined to wield the Master Sword", and "want": "To save his uncle, rescue Zelda, and ultimately prevent Aghanim and Ganon from destroying Hyrule.". This slide provides further concrete details of the structured data generated by the 'Premise Agent'.

The slide is titled "Premise.json" in large blue text at the top. Below the title, a code block on a dark background displays a further continuation of the example `premise.json` file from the previous two slides. This portion of the JSON object completes the "protagonists" array, showing the closing bracket for the first protagonist (Link) defined on the previous slide. It then defines the "key_antagonist" object, which contains "name": "Aghanim", "role": "Evil sorcerer, Ganon's pawn", and "want": "To seal the seven maidens, including Princess Zelda, to open a portal to the Dark World and release Ganon.". Next, it defines the "key_ally" object, with "name": "Sahasrahla", "role": "Village elder, wise man, guide", and "want": "To guide Link to acquire the Master Sword and stop Aghanim's plans to release Ganon.". Finally, it shows the "stakes" key-value pair: "The total conquest of the Light World by the dark lord Ganon, resulting in the enslavement or destruction of its inhabitants.". This slide provides the concluding details of the structured data generated by the 'Premise Agent', completing the example of the movie's core concept.

The slide is titled "Arc Agent" in large blue text at the top. In the top right corner, a small image of the AWESOM-O robot from the first slide is visible. The slide contains four main bullet points describing the function and output of the Arc Agent, which follows the Premise Agent in the screenplay generation flow. The first bullet states, "A chat agent that discusses your movie story arc," followed by two sub-bullets: "– Arc consists of multiple acts" and "– Agent takes premise.json as input for context." The second bullet point explains, "You tell the agent what happens in each act, then ask it to draft the act, one at a time." The third bullet reiterates, "Premise file gives it context for the story arc." The final bullet point specifies the agent's output: "This agent then creates a file arc.json." This slide details the next step in the hierarchical screenplay generation process, building upon the `premise.json` output from the previous slides.

The slide is titled "Arc.json" in large blue text at the top. Below the title, a code block on a dark background displays the contents of an example `arc.json` file. This JSON object represents a single act within a movie's story arc, specifically 'Act 3'. It includes several key-value pairs: "act": 3, "label": "Battle Aghanim and the Dark World", "approx_pages_note": "This act, encompassing the climax and a major cliffhanger, will likely be around 20-30 pages of a 120-page screenplay.", "dramatic_question": "Can Link defeat Aghanim, save Zelda and the other maidens, and prevent Ganon from conquering their world, even when cast into the heart of darkness?". A detailed "summary" describes Link's journey to find the Master Sword, rescue Zelda, confront Aghanim, and his banishment to the Dark World. The "turning_point_end" is described as "Aghanim banishes Link to the corrupted Dark World, setting the stage for a new, desperate quest as the ultimate evil, Ganon, looms large.". An array named "must_hit_beats" lists specific plot points for this act, such as Link being guided to the Master Sword, finding it, Zelda's telepathic contact, Link's return to Hyrule Castle, confronting Aghanim, Aghanim's plan to unleash Ganon, their battle, Link's banishment, his landing on the Great Pyramid, and Sahasrahla's instructions. Finally, "midpoint_note" is null. This slide provides a concrete example of the output from the 'Arc Agent' discussed in the previous slide, detailing a specific act of the screenplay's story arc.

The slide is titled "Sequence Agent" in large blue text at the top. In the top right corner, a small image of the AWESOM-O robot from the first slide is visible. The slide contains four main bullet points describing the function and output of the Sequence Agent, which follows the Arc Agent in the screenplay generation flow. The first bullet states, "An automated agent (no chat) that writes the sequences of the movie for each act." The second bullet, "Inputs per sequence," has two sub-bullets: "– Premise.json, arc.json – give agent global view of the movie" and "– All previous sequences generated so far – give agent local context for what should happen next." The third bullet, "Primary sequence elements," lists three sub-bullets: "– Goal – what is the narrative purpose of this sequence," "– Key locations – locations in the sequence," and "– Exit beat – how the sequence ends and transitions to next sequence." The final bullet point specifies the agent's output: "This agent then creates a file sequence.json." This slide details the next step in the hierarchical screenplay generation process, building upon the `arc.json` output from the previous slide and preparing for the generation of detailed sequences.

The slide is titled "Sequence.json" in large blue text at the top. Below the title, a code block on a dark background displays the contents of an example `sequence.json` file. This JSON object contains a `project_id`, a `sequence_count` of 8, and a `note` (which is null). The core of the file is an array named `sequences`, which currently shows one entry. This entry, with `id`: "SEQ1", represents a single sequence within 'Act 1' of the movie. It has a `title`: "Zelda's Urgent Plea", a `goal`: "To establish Link's call to adventure, his initial motivation (saving his uncle and Zelda), and to equip him with his first tools for the perilous journey ahead.", and an `emotional_tone`: "Urgent, sorrowful, determined". The `key_locations` for this sequence are listed as "Link's Home" and "Hyrule Castle (Outer Walls)". The `exit_beat` is described as: "Armed with his uncle's sword and shield, Link steels himself, ready to infiltrate the formidable Hyrule Castle and rescue Princess Zelda." This slide provides a concrete example of the output generated by the 'Sequence Agent' discussed in the previous slide, illustrating the detailed breakdown of a movie's narrative into individual sequences.

The slide is titled "Scene Agent" in large blue text at the top. In the top right corner, a small image of the AWESOM-O robot from the first slide is visible. The slide contains four main bullet points describing the function and output of the Scene Agent, which is the final step in the screenplay generation flow. The first bullet states, "An automated agent (no chat) that writes the scenes of the movie for each sequence." The second bullet, "Inputs per sequence," has two sub-bullets: "– Premise.json, arc.json, sequence.json – give agent global view of the movie" and "– All scenes generated so far in this sequence and the previous sequence – give agent local context for what should happen next." The third bullet, "Primary scene elements," lists two sub-bullets: "– Slug line – location of the scene" and "– Blocks – series of actions and dialogues in the scene." The final bullet point specifies the agent's output: "This agent then creates a file scenes.json." This slide details the most granular step in the hierarchical screenplay generation process, building upon the `sequence.json` output from the previous slide and completing the set of JSON outputs for the entire screenplay.

The slide is titled "Scenes.json" in large blue text at the top. Below the title, a code block on a dark background displays the beginning of an example `scenes.json` file. This JSON object contains an array named `scenes`, with the first entry representing a single scene. This scene has an `id`: "S0001", `slug_id`: "SEQ1-01", `slugline`: "INT. LINK'S HOME - NIGHT", and `sequence_id`: "SEQ1". The core of the scene is an array of `blocks`, which detail actions and dialogue. The visible blocks include three 'action' blocks describing Link sleeping, hearing a whisper, and waking up. Following these is a 'dialogue' block where the `character` is "ZELDA (V.O.)" and the `lines` are "Link... help me... I'm trapped in the castle... Aghanim... has me...". The final visible block is another 'action' block describing Link clutching his head as visions of Hyrule Castle, Zelda, and a sorcerer flash through his mind. This slide provides a concrete example of the detailed scene output generated by the 'Scene Agent' discussed in the previous slide, illustrating the granular breakdown of a screenplay into individual actions and dialogues.

The slide is titled "Enhancement Agents" in large blue text at the top. It introduces a concept for improving the previously described agentic flow for screenplay generation. A main bullet point states, "We can improve the agentic flow by using agents to enhance intermediate outputs." This is followed by three sub-bullets detailing these enhancements: "– Write better sequences or scenes," "– Clean up the premise," and "– Make the arc more creative." To the right of the text, a golden robot, similar to the AWESOM-O robot seen in earlier slides, is depicted, with a label on its chest reading "ENHANCEMENT AGENT." Below the text and robot, a modified version of the "Screenplay Flow" diagram from slide 5 is presented. This diagram shows the blue "Premise" box, purple "Arc" box, orange "Sequences" box, and green "Scenes" box, each with their respective JSON filenames. Crucially, small images of the "ENHANCEMENT AGENT" robot are now placed between the stages of the flow, indicating their role in refining the output. Specifically, an enhancement agent is shown between "Premise" and "Arc," another between "Arc" and "Sequences," and a third between "Arc" and "Scenes." This visually demonstrates how these new agents integrate into the existing hierarchical generation process to improve the quality of the intermediate outputs.

The slide is titled "Generalizing to Other Documents" in large blue text. Below the title, a single bullet point states: "By modifying the agent prompts, this hierarchical flow can be used for other long text documents." The main content of the slide is a table with six columns: "Document", "Premise", "Arc", "Sequence", "Scene", and "Artist Rendering". This table illustrates how the hierarchical structure used for screenplays can be adapted to various other document types. Each row represents a different document type:

1.  **Book:** Premise, Table of contents, Chapter, Sub-chapter. The 'Artist Rendering' column shows an image of the AWESOM-O robot sitting at a desk with a book labeled "BOOK".
2.  **Podcast:** Concept, Flow, format, Segment, Beat. The 'Artist Rendering' column shows the AWESOM-O robot in a recording studio with a microphone.
3.  **Song:** Premise, Structure (verse, hooks, coda), Verse, Couplet. The 'Artist Rendering' column shows the AWESOM-O robot on a stage with musical instruments and stage lights.
4.  **Lecture:** Learning objective, Outline, Module, Slide. The 'Artist Rendering' column shows the AWESOM-O robot in a classroom, standing next to a whiteboard with a diagram.
5.  **Business plan:** Value prop, Outline, Section, Sub-section. The 'Artist Rendering' column shows the AWESOM-O robot in an office, looking at a document labeled "BUSINESS PLAN".

This slide expands on the concept of agentic flows for long document generation, demonstrating the versatility of the hierarchical approach beyond screenplays, as initially mentioned in slide 2.

The slide is titled "Coding Session" in large blue text at the top. Below the title, three bullet points outline practical activities related to the lecture's content:
- "Write a short screenplay using Awesome-O"
- "Add enhancement agents to the agentic flow"
- "Modify Awesome-O for different document types"

Below the bullet points, a simplified flowchart diagram, similar to the "Screenplay Flow" from slide 5, is presented. It illustrates the core hierarchical process for generating a screenplay, with small AWESOM-O robot icons placed on top of each stage and on the connecting arrows, symbolizing the agents at work. The flow consists of four colored rectangular boxes:
- A blue box labeled "Premise" with "premise.json" below it.
- A purple box labeled "Arc" with "arc.json" below it.
- An orange box labeled "Sequences" with "sequence.json" below it.
- A green box labeled "Scenes" with "scenes.json" below it.

Arrows indicate the flow: from "Premise" to "Arc," and then branching from "Arc" to both "Sequences" and "Scenes." This slide serves as a summary of the practical application of the AI agentic flow for screenplay generation, enhancement, and generalization, discussed in the preceding slides.
